name: Truth
definition: Truth is the quality of being in accordance with fact, reality, evidence, or verified understanding.
sourceRootNote: Truth is not just what is said. In SourceRoot, truth is what can be traced, sourced, revised, and understood through its relationships.
originLanguage: Old English: faithfulness, loyalty, firmness, or trustworthiness.
conceptualOrigin: The modern concept of truth connects factual accuracy, trust, reality, verification, and knowledge.

timeline.title: Faithfulness and loyalty
timeline.description: Truth was closely tied to trust, promise-keeping, fidelity, and reliability.

timeline.title: Honesty and sincerity
timeline.description: The meaning expanded toward truthful speech and personal integrity.

timeline.title: Conformity with reality or fact
timeline.description: Truth became closely connected to evidence, verification, logic, and objective reality.

related.name: Trust
related.description: Truth and trust share historical and conceptual roots.

related.name: Reality
related.description: Truth corresponds to what is real or actual.

related.name: Belief
related.description: Belief may align with, diverge from, or seek truth.

revisions.description: Connected Truth to Evidence, Verification, Knowledge, Trust, and Reality.

name: Knowledge
definition: Knowledge is understanding, awareness, or familiarity gained through experience, study, evidence, or reasoning.
sourceRootNote: Knowledge becomes stronger when its sources, relationships, and revisions can be traced.
originLanguage: Old English: to know, perceive, recognize, or understand.
conceptualOrigin: Knowledge connects belief, truth, evidence, learning, memory, and verification.

timeline.title: Knowledge as justified understanding
timeline.description: Knowledge was often examined through truth, belief, reason, and justification.

timeline.title: Knowledge through observation and evidence
timeline.description: Knowledge became increasingly tied to repeatable methods, evidence, and verification.

timeline.title: Knowledge as sourced and traceable information
timeline.description: In a digital world, knowledge requires provenance, context, and source history.

related.name: Belief
related.description: Knowledge is often distinguished from belief.

related.name: Verification
related.description: Knowledge can be tested and confirmed.

name: Trust
definition: Trust is confidence in the reliability, truth, ability, or integrity of a person, source, system, or claim.
sourceRootNote: Trust is not blind belief. In SourceRoot, trust is strengthened by transparent sources, revision history, and contributor identity.
originLanguage: Old English: faithful, loyal, firm, or trustworthy.
conceptualOrigin: Trust connects truth, reliability, reputation, verification, and relationship history.

timeline.title: Faithfulness and loyalty
timeline.description: Trust was rooted in reliability, promise-keeping, and faithfulness.

timeline.title: Confidence in reliability
timeline.description: Trust expanded to include confidence in people, institutions, sources, and systems.

related.name: Truth
related.description: Trust and truth share historical and conceptual roots.

related.name: Source
related.description: Trust depends on source quality and transparency.

name: Evidence
definition: Evidence is information, observation, testimony, data, or material that supports or challenges a claim.
sourceRootNote: Evidence is one of the core bridges between a claim and its confidence.
conceptualOrigin: Evidence connects claims, sources, observations, verification, and conclusions.

timeline.title: That which is clear or visible
timeline.description: Evidence originally connected to what could be seen, shown, or made clear.

timeline.title: Support for claims
timeline.description: Evidence became central in science, law, research, and argumentation.

name: Verification
definition: Verification is the process of checking, confirming, or proving that something is accurate, authentic, or true.
sourceRootNote: Verification turns a claim from isolated information into something that can be evaluated and trusted.
conceptualOrigin: Verification connects truth, evidence, method, source quality, and confidence.

timeline.title: To make true or confirm
timeline.description: Verification developed from roots connected to truth and confirmation.

timeline.title: Checking accuracy and authenticity
timeline.description: Verification is now used across science, identity, media, software, and records.

name: Reality
definition: Reality is the state of things as they actually exist, independent of appearance, belief, or interpretation.
sourceRootNote: Reality is the anchor that truth claims attempt to describe.
conceptualOrigin: Reality connects existence, truth, perception, evidence, and interpretation.

timeline.title: Being and existence
timeline.description: Reality has long been studied through questions of what exists and how it can be known.

timeline.title: Actual existence
timeline.description: Reality is commonly contrasted with illusion, belief, fiction, or appearance.

related.name: Belief
related.description: Belief may or may not match reality.

name: Belief
definition: Belief is acceptance that something is true, real, or meaningful, with or without complete proof.
sourceRootNote: Belief is important because it can align with truth, conflict with truth, or motivate the search for truth.
originLanguage: Old English: belief, faith, trust, or confidence.
conceptualOrigin: Belief connects trust, faith, knowledge, evidence, and interpretation.

timeline.title: Faith and trust
timeline.description: Belief was closely connected to faith, loyalty, and confidence.

timeline.title: Acceptance of a claim
timeline.description: Belief came to mean accepting something as true or real.

related.name: Truth
related.description: Belief may seek or claim truth.

related.name: Knowledge
related.description: Knowledge is often distinguished from belief.

related.name: Trust
related.description: Belief can be shaped by trust.
